rows.label: When the agent is unknown
items: A rare manuscript **was stolen** from the university archive overnight.

rows.label: When the agent is obvious
items: The emergency bridge **was reopened** at dawn after final checks.

rows.label: When the action matters more than the doer
items: Several safety recommendations **were introduced** after the inspection.

rows.label: To highlight new information at the end
items: The final decision **was announced** by the board chair at noon.

rows.label: To avoid heavy subjects at the start
items: We **were impressed** by the number of volunteers joining the campaign.

rows.label: To create a formal register
items: All candidates **are required** to carry photographic identification.

rows: Noun + passive verb + infinitive / perfect infinitive
believe, expect, find, know, report, say, think, understand
Renewable energy **is expected to account** for most new electricity capacity this decade.
There + passive verb + infinitive / perfect infinitive
believe, consider, estimate, report, say, think
There **are reported to have been** significant delays at the border this morning.
It + passive verb + that-clause
accept, agree, assume, believe, claim, suggest, think
It **is widely believed that** early intervention improves long-term outcomes.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: Use the **perfect infinitive** (*to have + past participle*) for an action earlier than the reporting verb.
This structure is common in formal writing, reports and news summaries.

rows: Verb + indirect object + direct object
The committee offered the researcher a grant.
The researcher **was offered** a grant. / A grant **was offered** to the researcher.
Verb + direct object + to + indirect object
The editor sent a revised contract to the writer.
The writer **was sent** a revised contract. / A revised contract **was sent** to the writer.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: With **explain** and **suggest**, keep the preposition or clause structure: *The rule was explained to us*.
Not every ditransitive verb allows both passive patterns in natural use.

content.subtitle: Tenses usually avoided in the passive
content.description: Present perfect continuous, past perfect continuous, future continuous and future perfect continuous are rarely used in the passive; instead, fixed expressions with *be + prepositional phrase* are preferred.
rows: in progress
ongoing activity
Restoration work on the theatre roof **has been in progress** since January.
in training
long preparation period
By graduation, Maya **will have been in training** as a pilot for four years.
on display
public exhibition
Original sketches from the project **have been on display** all week.
under consideration
decision-making stage
The revised policy **has been under consideration** since last month.
under construction
building/development process
The coastal rail extension **has been under construction** for nearly two years.
Arranging services
have/get + object + past participle
We **had** the solar panels **installed** before winter.
Unwanted experiences
have/get + object + past participle
She **had** her phone **stolen** on the way home.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: You can combine this pattern with **need / want / would like**: *I need my laptop repaired today*.
**Get something done** can also mean finishing a task: *I got the report written before lunch*.

sections.title: Causative: get sb to do / have sb do

rows: get + person + to-infinitive
persuade or arrange for someone to do something
Could you **get Leo to review** the final draft?
have + person + bare infinitive
instruct someone to do something (often more formal/direct)
The supervisor **had the team update** the safety log immediately.
get + person + -ing
make someone start doing something (often by encouragement)
The coach **got everyone warming up** before sunrise.
have + person + -ing
cause someone to be in the middle of an activity
By noon, she **had the interns organising** all the files by priority.
have + thing + -ing
set a process in motion
We soon **had the new platform running** without errors.
